content.full_text:
October 22, 1993

R. Zaiden Corrado
13200 Crossroads Parkway North
Suite 345
City of Industry, CA 91746-3420

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File A-93-381

Dear Mr. Corrado:

As the City Attorney for the city of Adelanto, you have
requested advice on behalf of Mr. Ernest Scott, a member of the
City Council of Adelanto, regarding his duties under the conflict-
of-interest provision of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").1

QUESTION

Councilmember Scott has a financial interest in the sale of
certain properties, but is not involved in any decisionmaking with
respect to these properties. Does Councilmember Scott have a
conflict of interest under the Act?

CONCLUSION

Under the Act, Councilmember Scott does not have a conflict
of interest if he does not make, participate in making or use his
official position to influence a governmental decision regarding
the properties.

FACTS

Mr. Scott is a licensed real estate broker, who has an
exclusive listing to sell certain subdivided properties within the
city of Adelanto. No zoning change or other city council action
will be required to affect the sale of these subdivided
properties. The only regulatory action required will be a lot
split and the preparation of an environmental impact report
("EIR"). Both the lot split and the EIR fall under the purview of
the planning commission. Mr. Scott is not involved in the
decisionmaking of the planning commission.

1 Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission
regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations, Sections
18000-18954. All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento, CA 95804-0807 • (916) 322-5660

File No. A-93-381
Page 2


ANALYSIS

Section 87100 prohibits a public official from making,
participating in making, or using his or her official position to
influence a governmental decision in which the official knows or
has reason to know the official has a financial interest.

An official has a financial interest in a governmental deci-
sion within the meaning of Section 87100 if it is reasonably
foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial ef-
fect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on
the official, or on a member of the official's immediate family,
or on:

(a) Any business entity in which the public
official has a direct or indirect investment worth
one thousand dollars ($1,000) or more.

(b) Any real property in which the public
official has a direct or indirect interest worth
one thousand dollars ($1,000) or more.

(c) Any source of income, other than gifts
and other than loans by a commercial lending
institution in the regular course of business on
terms available to the public without regard to
official status, aggregating two hundred fifty dol-
lars ($250) or more in value provided to, received
by or promised to the public official within 12
months prior to the time when the decision is made.

(d) Any business entity in which the public
official is a director, officer, partner, trustee,
employee, or holds any position of management.

Section 87103(a)-(d).

You have stated that Councilmember Scott has a financial
interest in decisions that will be made by the planning
commission. However, since the city council will not be making
any decisions regarding the properties, Councilmember Scott does
not have a conflict of interest under the Act provided that he
does not use his official position to influence any planning
commission decisions regarding the properties (Regulation
18700.1). In addition, the planning commission decisions must be
final decisions which are not reviewed by the city council.

Should the facts of Mr. Scott's situation change, this advice
must be reevaluated. You may recontact us at such time.

File No. A-93-381
Page 3


I trust this answers your question.


Sincerely,

Steven G. Churchwell
General Counsel

By: [illegible]
Counsel, Legal Division

SGC:JS:ak
